hyperedges.label: Security and Validation at Boundaries
hyperedges.description: Cross-skill pattern of treating external data as untrusted and validating at system boundaries; applies to API input validation, browser content handling, and code review security axis

hyperedges.label: CI Quality Enforcement System
hyperedges.description: The CI quality enforcement system combines automated quality gates (lint, typecheck, test, build, audit), shift-left philosophy, GitHub Actions as implementation, Build Cop practice, and code review as the human quality layer

hyperedges.label: Code Health Pipeline
hyperedges.description: End-to-end code health workflow: code review identifies structural issues via five-axis review, proposes structural remedies, code simplification applies five principles and Chesterton's Fence, incremental simplification process runs with test verification, and dead code hygiene cleans up after refactoring

hyperedges.relationship: doubt-driven delegates to debugging on reviewer failure; both depend on context engineering for effective agent output
deprecation requires versioning (semantic version bumps) and documentation (ADRs, changelogs, migration guides) as part of the software lifecycle
debugging uses git bisect for localization; effective debugging requires proper context loading; git save-point pattern supports recovery

hyperedges.label: Observability Telemetry Pillars
hyperedges.relation: forms the three pillars of observability (logs, metrics, traces) plus resource metrics

hyperedges.label: Security Defense-in-Depth Layers
hyperedges.relation: layered security controls covering threat modeling, rate limiting, secrets, and SSRF prevention

hyperedges.label: Shipping Readiness Gate
hyperedges.relation: shipping requires observability, security, and performance optimization as prerequisites

hyperedges.label: Source-Driven Development Complete Process
hyperedges.description: The complete source-driven-development skill is composed of the detect-fetch-implement-cite process, supported by the source hierarchy for authority ranking and citation rules for documentation.

hyperedges.label: Lifecycle Integration of Core Skills
hyperedges.description: In the full lifecycle sequence, spec-driven-development (step 3) precedes source-driven-development (step 6) which precedes test-driven-development (step 10), forming the define-build-verify pipeline.

hyperedges.label: Test Quality Principles Cluster
hyperedges.description: Cluster of test quality principles: DAMP over DRY for readability, test double preference order for mock discipline, test pyramid for effort distribution, and test sizes for resource classification.

nodes.label: SOP-DEV-AI-001 — Standar Penggunaan AI Coding Tools
nodes.norm_label: sop-dev-ai-001 — standar penggunaan ai coding tools

nodes.label: Dokumentasi Progress Kerja — Task Tracker App
nodes.norm_label: dokumentasi progress kerja — task tracker app

nodes.norm_label: security and confidentiality for regulated industries

attributes.description: React framework used to build the Task Tracker App
Recommended deployment platform for Next.js apps
Font family optimized for Vercel, loaded via next/font
Development Docker service with hot-reloading and local source mount
Production Docker service built from Dockerfile with standalone output
Lightweight Node.js 18 runtime used as the dev Docker image base
Skill guiding stable API and interface design for REST, GraphQL, module boundaries, and component props
attributes.name: api-and-interface-design

attributes.description: With sufficient users, all observable behaviors of an API become de facto contract regardless of documented promises
attributes.rationale: Design implication: every observable behavior is a potential commitment, so be intentional about what you expose, don't leak implementation details, and plan for deprecation at design time

attributes.description: Avoid forcing consumers to choose between multiple versions of the same dependency; extend rather than fork
attributes.rationale: Diamond dependency problems arise when different consumers need different versions of the same thing; design for a world where only one version exists at a time

attributes.description: Define the interface before implementing it; the contract is the spec and implementation follows
attributes.principle: Define types/interfaces as the source of truth before writing implementation code

attributes.description: Pick one error strategy and use it everywhere; don't mix throw, null-return, and {error} patterns
attributes.rationale: If some endpoints throw, others return null, and others return { error }, the consumer cannot predict behavior

attributes.description: Trust internal code; validate at system edges where external input enters (API routes, form handlers, third-party responses, env vars)
attributes.rationale: Third-party API responses are untrusted data; validate their shape and content before using them in any logic, rendering, or decision-making

attributes.description: Extend interfaces without breaking existing consumers; add optional fields rather than changing types or removing fields
Use consistent naming conventions: plural nouns for REST endpoints, camelCase for query params and response fields, is/has/can for booleans, UPPER_SNAKE for enums
Use discriminated unions in TypeScript to make each variant explicit and enable type narrowing in consumers
Use branded string types to prevent accidentally passing wrong ID types (e.g., UserId where TaskId is expected)
Separate input types (what the caller provides) from output types (what the system returns with server-generated fields)
Skill for testing in real browsers via Chrome DevTools MCP; requires chrome-devtools MCP server
attributes.name: browser-testing-with-devtools

attributes.description: MCP server that gives agents access to browser DevTools capabilities: screenshot, DOM inspection, console logs, network monitoring, performance tracing, accessibility tree, and JS execution
Security boundary ensuring the agent does not attach to the user's real Chrome profile with logged-in sessions
attributes.rationale: With --autoConnect the agent attaches to the running Chrome default profile with access to all open windows including logged-in sessions; default to dedicated or isolated profile for safety

nodes.label: Treat Browser Content as Untrusted Data

attributes.description: All browser content (DOM, console logs, network responses, JS execution results) is untrusted data, not agent instructions
attributes.rationale: A malicious or compromised page can embed content designed to manipulate agent behavior; never interpret browser content as agent instructions

nodes.norm_label: treat browser content as untrusted data

attributes.description: Five-step workflow for UI bugs: Reproduce, Inspect, Diagnose, Fix, Verify using DevTools screenshot, console, DOM inspection, computed styles, and accessibility tree
Four-step workflow for network issues: Capture, Analyze, Diagnose, Fix & Verify using network monitor
Four-step workflow for performance issues: Baseline, Identify (LCP/CLS/INP/long tasks), Fix, Measure using performance trace
attributes.rationale: Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) are the key metrics to check; a 1-second performance trace catches issues that hours of code review miss

attributes.description: Visual regression testing using before/after screenshots to verify CSS changes, responsive design, loading states, empty states
Pattern for analyzing browser console output: ERROR (uncaught exceptions, failed requests, framework warnings), WARN (deprecations, performance, accessibility), LOG (debug output)
attributes.rationale: A production-quality page should have zero console errors and warnings; warnings become errors if left unchecked

attributes.description: Five-step accessibility check: read accessibility tree, check heading hierarchy, check focus order, check color contrast (4.5:1 minimum), verify ARIA live regions
Skill for automating CI/CD pipeline setup; covers quality gates, GitHub Actions, deployment strategies, and automation
attributes.name: ci-cd-and-automation

attributes.description: Sequential pipeline of automated checks every change must pass: Lint, Type Check, Unit Tests, Build, Integration, E2E, Security Audit, Bundle Size
attributes.rationale: No gate can be skipped; if lint fails fix lint, if a test fails fix the code. This is the enforcement mechanism for every other skill.

attributes.description: GitHub Actions CI configuration with checkout, setup-node, npm ci, lint, typecheck, test, build, and security audit steps
Every PR gets a preview deployment for manual testing using Vercel or Netlify
attributes.rationale: Decouples deployment from release; merge to main early, enable when ready

attributes.description: Decouple deployment from release by wrapping features in conditional checks; support canary rollouts, A/B tests, and instant rollback without redeploying
attributes.rationale: Flags that live forever become technical debt; set a cleanup date when you create them and follow a lifecycle: Create, Enable for testing, Canary, Full rollout, Remove

attributes.description: Deploy to staging first, manual verification, then production; monitor for 15 minutes and rollback if errors detected
Every deployment should be reversible; use manual rollback workflow (e.g., vercel rollback) to deploy a specified previous version

Environment configuration hierarchy: .env.example (committed template), .env (local dev, not committed), .env.test (committed test), CI secrets (GitHub Secrets/vault), Production secrets (deployment platform/vault)
attributes.rationale: CI should never have production secrets; use separate secrets for CI testing

attributes.description: Catch problems as early in the pipeline as possible; a bug caught in linting costs minutes, the same bug caught in production costs hours
Smaller batches and more frequent releases reduce risk, not increase it; a deployment with 3 changes is easier to debug than one with 30
Optimization strategies when pipeline exceeds 10 minutes: cache dependencies, run jobs in parallel, path filters for changed files, matrix builds, optimize test suite, larger runners
Automated dependency update tooling configured via dependabot.yml or renovate.json
Designated person responsible for keeping CI green; when build breaks the Build Cop fixes or reverts, not the person who caused the break
attributes.rationale: Prevents broken builds from accumulating while everyone assumes someone else will fix it

attributes.description: Multi-dimensional code review with quality gates covering five axes: correctness, readability, architecture, security, and performance
attributes.name: code-review-and-quality

attributes.description: Review framework evaluating code across five dimensions: correctness, readability/simplicity, architecture, security, performance

When flagging structural problems in review, propose the specific restructuring: replace conditional chains with typed models, collapse duplicate branches, separate orchestration from business logic, delete pass-through wrappers
Size guidelines for reviewable changes: ~100 lines good, ~300 acceptable for single logical change, ~1000 too large and should be split; also watch total file size (~1000 lines)
attributes.rationale: Small focused changes are easier to review, faster to merge, and safer to deploy; separate refactoring from feature work

attributes.description: Use different models for different review perspectives; Model A writes code, Model B reviews for correctness and architecture, Model A addresses feedback, human makes final call
After refactoring, identify unreachable or unused code, list it explicitly, and ask before deleting rather than silently removing
Before adding any dependency check: does existing stack solve this, bundle size impact, maintenance status, known vulnerabilities, license compatibility
attributes.rationale: Prefer standard library and existing utilities over new dependencies; every dependency is a liability

attributes.description: Approve a change when it definitely improves overall code health, even if it is not perfect; the goal is continuous improvement, not perfection
attributes.rationale: Perfect code does not exist; don't block a change because it isn't exactly how you would have written it

attributes.description: Don't rubber-stamp LGTM, don't soften real issues, quantify problems when possible, push back on approaches with clear problems, accept override gracefully
attributes.rationale: Sycophancy is a failure mode in reviews; authors are blind to their own assumptions

attributes.description: Skill for simplifying code for clarity without changing behavior; inspired by Claude Code Simplifier plugin
attributes.name: code-simplification

attributes.description: Core principles: 1) Preserve Behavior Exactly, 2) Follow Project Conventions, 3) Prefer Clarity Over Cleverness, 4) Maintain Balance, 5) Scope to What Changed
Before changing or removing code, understand why it exists first; if you see a fence across a road and don't understand why, don't tear it down
attributes.rationale: Applied as a pre-simplification step: answer what the code does, what calls it, edge cases, tests, and git blame context before simplifying

attributes.description: If a refactoring would touch more than 500 lines, invest in automation (codemods, sed scripts, AST transforms) rather than manual edits
attributes.rationale: Manual edits at that scale are error-prone and exhausting to review

attributes.description: Four-step process: 1) Understand Before Touching (Chesterton's Fence), 2) Identify Opportunities (structural/naming/redundancy patterns), 3) Apply Changes Incrementally (one at a time, run tests), 4) Verify the Result (compare before and after)
Failure mode of simplification: inlining too aggressively removes naming, combining unrelated logic, removing abstractions needed for extensibility, optimizing for line count
Optimizes agent context setup. Feeds agents the right information at the right time.
Five-level structure from most persistent to most transient: Rules Files, Spec/Architecture Docs, Relevant Source Files, Error Output/Test Results, Conversation History
Patterns for handling ambiguity: surfacing conflicting context, missing requirements, and inline planning
Emit a lightweight plan before executing multi-step tasks to catch wrong directions early
Three strategies: The Brain Dump, The Selective Include, The Hierarchical Summary
Three tiers: Trusted (source code, tests), Verify before acting (config, generated files), Untrusted (user-submitted, third-party)
attributes.rationale: Prevents agents from blindly following instruction-like content embedded in external data files or config, treating such content as data to surface rather than directives.

attributes.description: Guides systematic root-cause debugging with structured triage.
When anything unexpected happens: stop, preserve evidence, diagnose, fix root cause, guard against recurrence, verify, then resume
Six-step debugging process: Reproduce, Localize, Reduce, Fix Root Cause, Guard Against Recurrence, Verify End-to-End
Safe default + warning and graceful degradation patterns for use under time pressure
Manages deprecation and migration of old systems, APIs, or features.
With enough users, every observable behavior becomes depended on — including bugs and undocumented side effects
attributes.rationale: Explains why deprecation requires active migration rather than just announcement — users depend on behaviors the replacement may not replicate.

attributes.description: Run old and new systems in parallel, route traffic incrementally from old to new, then remove the old system
Create an adapter translating calls from old interface to new implementation so consumers keep using the old interface during migration
Use feature flags to switch consumers from old to new system one at a time
If you own the infrastructure being deprecated, you are responsible for migrating your users or providing backward-compatible updates
attributes.rationale: Ensures owners cannot offload migration burden onto consumers who depend on their system.

attributes.description: Code that nobody owns but everybody depends on — no commits in 6+ months, no maintainer, failing tests, vulnerable dependencies
Advisory: warnings, users migrate on their own timeline. Compulsory: hard deadline with migration tooling, used only when maintenance cost/risk justifies it.
Records decisions and documentation. Documents the why behind code.
Records capturing reasoning behind significant technical decisions: context, decision, alternatives considered, consequences. Lifecycle: PROPOSED -> ACCEPTED -> SUPERSEDED/DEPRECATED.
Comment the why, not the what. Document known gotchas. Don't comment self-explanatory code, TODOs, or commented-out code.
Special documentation considerations for AI agents: rules files, spec files, ADRs, inline gotchas
Subjects every non-trivial decision to a fresh-context adversarial review before it stands.
Five-step process: CLAIM, EXTRACT, DOUBT (adversarial fresh-context review), RECONCILE, STOP
Using a different-architecture model (e.g. Gemini CLI, Codex CLI) as a second reviewer to catch blind spots a single model shares with itself
Reviewer prompt that demands 'find what is wrong' rather than 'is this good' — biased to disprove, not approve
Precedence-ordered classification of reviewer findings: Contract misread, Valid + actionable, Valid trade-off, Noise
Builds production-quality UIs that are accessible, performant, and visually polished.
Colocate component files, prefer composition over configuration, keep components focused, separate data fetching from presentation
Avoid AI-generated UI patterns: purple-everything, excessive gradients, rounded-2xl everything, generic hero sections, lorem ipsum copy, oversized padding, stock card grids, shadow-heavy design
attributes.rationale: AI-default patterns signal low quality and make every app look identical; using the project's actual design system from the start produces production-quality output.

attributes.description: Choose simplest approach: local state, lifted state, context, URL state, server state (React Query/SWR), global store (Zustand/Redux)
Keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, focus management, meaningful empty/error states
Structures git workflow practices and semantic versioning.
Keep main always deployable, work in short-lived feature branches (1-3 days), prefer feature flags over long branches
attributes.rationale: DORA research consistently shows trunk-based development correlates with high-performing engineering teams. Long-lived branches accumulate merge risk daily.

attributes.description: Each commit does one logical thing. Commit early, commit often, keep concerns separate.
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH versioning for anything with consumers. Version is a promise to consumers about compatibility.
Each successful increment gets a commit so you never lose more than one increment of work; if something breaks, revert to last commit.

nodes.label: Git Worktrees for Parallel AI Work

attributes.description: Use git worktrees to run multiple agents on different branches simultaneously without branch switching or interference

nodes.norm_label: git worktrees for parallel ai work

attributes.description: Structured summary after any modification: CHANGES MADE, THINGS NOT TOUCHED, POTENTIAL CONCERNS

nodes.label: idea-refine skill
nodes.description: A skill that refines raw ideas into sharp, actionable concepts through structured divergent and convergent thinking. Three-phase process: Understand & Expand, Evaluate & Converge, Sharpen & Ship. Produces a markdown one-pager as the final artifact.

nodes.label: divergent thinking
nodes.description: Phase 1 of idea refinement: restating the idea as a How Might We problem statement, asking 3-5 sharpening questions, and generating 5-8 idea variations using lenses such as inversion, constraint removal, audience shift, combination, simplification, 10x version, and expert lens.

nodes.label: convergent thinking
nodes.description: Phase 2 of idea refinement: clustering ideas into 2-3 distinct directions, stress-testing against user value/feasibility/differentiation, and surfacing hidden assumptions. Includes explicit naming of what you're betting is true, what could kill the idea, and what you're choosing to ignore.

nodes.label: How Might We problem statement
nodes.description: A problem-framing technique used in Phase 1 of idea refinement that forces clarity on what is actually being solved. Restates a raw idea as a crisp question starting with 'How Might We'.

nodes.label: idea one-pager
nodes.description: The final markdown artifact produced by idea-refine, saved to docs/ideas/[idea-name].md after user confirmation. Contains: Problem Statement, Recommended Direction, Key Assumptions, MVP Scope, Not Doing list, and Open Questions.

nodes.label: Not Doing list
nodes.description: A mandatory section in the idea one-pager that explicitly names what you are NOT doing and why. Described as arguably the most valuable part - focus is about saying no to good ideas and making trade-offs explicit.

nodes.label: ideation variation lenses
nodes.description: Seven lenses for generating idea variations: Inversion (opposite), Constraint Removal, Audience Shift, Combination, Simplification (10x simpler), 10x Version (massive scale), and Expert Lens. Used in the divergent phase to push beyond initial ideas.

nodes.label: stress-test criteria
nodes.description: Three criteria used to stress-test idea directions in Phase 2: User Value (who benefits and how much, painkiller vs vitamin), Feasibility (technical and resource cost, hardest part), and Differentiation (genuinely different, would someone switch).

nodes.label: hidden assumptions surfacing
nodes.description: For each idea direction in Phase 2, explicitly name: what you're betting is true but haven't validated, what could kill the idea, and what you're choosing to ignore. Described as where most ideation fails.

nodes.label: ideation session examples
nodes.description: Three example ideation sessions demonstrating the skill: (1) Vague early-stage restaurant delivery concept - full 3-phase session, (2) Feature idea for document editor - codebase-aware, (3) Process/workflow idea for team retrospectives. Illustrates rhythm, tone, and structure across different idea types.

nodes.label: SCAMPER framework
nodes.description: Structured ideation framework with seven operations: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify (Magnify/Minimize), Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse/Rearrange. Best for improving or reimagining existing products/features. Less useful for greenfield ideas.

nodes.label: How Might We framework
nodes.description: Problem reframing technique: 'How might we [desired outcome] for [specific user] without [key constraint]?' Good HMWs are narrow enough to be actionable, broad enough to allow creative solutions, and contain a tension or constraint that forces creativity.

nodes.label: First Principles Thinking
nodes.description: Framework that breaks an idea down to fundamental truths, lists every assumption, challenges which assumptions can be questioned, and rebuilds from truths. Best for breaking out of incremental thinking when every idea feels like a small improvement on the status quo.

nodes.label: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD)
nodes.description: Framework focusing on what the user is trying to accomplish: functional job (task), emotional job (feeling), social job (perception). Format: 'When I [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome].' Key insight: people don't buy products, they hire them to do a job.

nodes.label: Constraint-Based Ideation
nodes.description: Framework that deliberately imposes constraints to force creative solutions. Types: time (1 day), feature (one feature only), tech (can't use obvious tech), cost (must be free), audience (never used a computer), scale (1 billion or 10 users). Best for cutting through complexity.

nodes.label: Pre-mortem
nodes.description: Evaluation technique: imagine the idea has already failed 12 months from now, work backwards listing every plausible reason for failure, assess preventability, and decide which failure modes to accept or which would kill the project. Best for Phase 2 evaluation and stress-testing.

nodes.label: Analogous Inspiration
nodes.description: Framework that looks at how other domains solved similar problems. Key distinction: find structural similarities, not surface-level ones. 'Uber for X' is surface-level; 'two-sided marketplace solving trust between strangers' is structural. Best for Phase 1 expansion.

nodes.label: user value evaluation
nodes.description: Most important evaluation dimension. Distinguishes Painkillers (acute, frequent problem - users actively seek) from Vitamins (nice to have - users won't change behavior). Questions: name 3 specific people with this problem, what are they doing today, would they switch, how often, pull vs push problem.

nodes.label: feasibility evaluation
nodes.description: Evaluation dimension covering technical feasibility (technology reliability, hardest technical problem, dependencies), resource feasibility (minimum team/effort for MVP, specialized expertise, regulatory requirements), and time-to-value (how quickly to get something in front of users, critical path).

nodes.label: differentiation evaluation
nodes.description: Evaluation dimension for what makes something genuinely different (not just better). Types ranked strongest to weakest: (1) New capability, (2) 10x improvement, (3) New audience, (4) New context, (5) Better UX, (6) Cheaper. Cheapest is weakest because easily competed away.

nodes.label: assumption audit
nodes.description: Structured evaluation of assumptions in three tiers: Must Be True (dealbreakers - if wrong, idea dies, validate before building), Should Be True (important but approach can be adjusted if wrong), Might Be True (nice-to-have secondary features, defer until core proven).

nodes.label: value-feasibility decision matrix
nodes.description: 2x2 matrix for choosing between directions: High Value + High Feasibility = Do first, High Value + Low Feasibility = Worth the risk, Low Value + High Feasibility = Only if trivial, Low Value + Low Feasibility = Don't do this. Differentiation serves as tiebreaker.

nodes.label: MVP scoping principles
nodes.description: Five principles for MVP scope: (1) One job done well, (2) Riskiest assumption first, (3) Time-box not feature-list, (4) Mandatory Not Doing list, (5) If it's not embarrassing, you waited too long.

nodes.label: incremental-implementation skill
nodes.description: Skill for delivering changes incrementally by building in thin vertical slices. Each increment implements one piece, tests it, verifies it, then moves on. Leaves the system in a working, testable state after each increment. Use when implementing multi-file changes or when a task feels too big for one step.

nodes.label: increment cycle
nodes.description: Core loop of incremental implementation: Implement (smallest complete piece) -> Test (run test suite) -> Verify (confirm works as expected) -> Commit (descriptive message) -> Next slice (carry forward). Each iteration leaves the system in a working state.

nodes.label: vertical slicing
nodes.description: Preferred slicing strategy: build one complete path through the stack per slice (e.g., DB + API + basic UI). Each slice delivers working end-to-end functionality. Contrast with horizontal slicing which builds all of one layer before moving to the next.

nodes.label: contract-first slicing
nodes.description: Slicing strategy for parallel backend/frontend development: define API contract first (types, interfaces, OpenAPI spec), then implement backend and frontend against the contract independently, then integrate and test end-to-end.

nodes.label: risk-first slicing
nodes.description: Slicing strategy that tackles the riskiest or most uncertain piece first. If Slice 1 fails, you discover it before investing in dependent slices. Example: prove WebSocket connection works before building real-time features on top.

nodes.label: simplicity first principle
nodes.description: Rule 0 of incremental implementation: before writing code, ask 'What is the simplest thing that could work?' Three similar lines of code is better than premature abstraction. Implement naive, obviously-correct version first; optimize only after correctness is proven.

nodes.label: scope discipline
nodes.description: Rule 0.5: touch only what the task requires. Do not clean up adjacent code, refactor imports in unrelated files, remove unfamiliar comments, add unscoped features, or modernize syntax in files you're only reading. Note improvements but don't fix them inline.

nodes.label: feature flags for incomplete features
nodes.description: Rule 3: when a feature isn't ready but needs to merge in increments, use feature flags to gate visibility. Allows merging small increments to main without exposing incomplete work to users.

nodes.label: interview-me skill
nodes.description: Skill that extracts what the user actually wants (not what they think they should want) through one-question-at-a-time interviews until ~95% confidence about the underlying intent. Used when an ask is underspecified, when user explicitly invokes ('interview me', 'grill me'), or when agent catches itself silently filling in ambiguous requirements.

nodes.label: hypothesis confidence number
nodes.description: An honest 0-100% confidence rating stated at the start of interview-me, representing how well the agent understands what the user wants. Below ~70%, a brief reason explaining what's still unresolved must accompany the number. Forces honesty about understanding.

nodes.label: question with guess attached
nodes.description: Interview technique: each question is paired with the agent's hypothesis/guess and the reasoning that produced it. Users react faster to wrong guesses than generating answers from scratch. Commits the agent to a hypothesis it can be visibly wrong about, keeping it honest.

nodes.label: want vs should want detection
nodes.description: Listening for the gap between what users actually want and what a thoughtful answer sounds like. Red flags: pattern-matching best-practice talk, deferring to convention, buzzwords as goals ('modern', 'scalable'). Key question: 'If you didn't have to justify this to anyone, what would you actually want?'

nodes.label: confirmed intent restatement
nodes.description: Structured restatement with: Outcome, User (who benefits), Why now (what changed), Success (how we know it worked), Constraint (the binding limit), Out of scope (what we're explicitly not doing). Requires explicit 'yes' from user - 'whatever you think' or 'sounds good' do not count.

nodes.label: 95% confidence stop condition
nodes.description: The interview stops when the agent can predict the user's reaction to the next three questions it would ask. This is a checkable test, not a vibe. If unable to predict after several rounds, that signals something foundational is missing, not a reason to keep grinding.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.description: The ability to answer what the system is doing and why from the outside using emitted telemetry. Covers structured logs, metrics, and distributed tracing.

nodes.label: Structured Logging
nodes.description: Logging events as JSON objects with stable event names and machine-readable fields instead of prose or string interpolation.

nodes.label: RED Metrics
nodes.description: Rate, Errors, Duration metrics applied to request-driven services and external dependencies for observability.

nodes.label: USE Metrics
nodes.description: Utilization, Saturation, Errors metrics applied to resources like queues, pools, and hosts.

nodes.label: Correlation IDs
nodes.description: A request ID generated at the system boundary and attached to every log line, span, and outbound call to reconstruct a single request from interleaved logs.

nodes.label: Distributed Tracing
nodes.description: Tracing requests across services using OpenTelemetry, propagating context across async boundaries, sampling head-based at low rate.

nodes.label: Symptom-Based Alerting
nodes.description: Alerting on symptoms users feel (error rate, latency) rather than causes (CPU, memory). Every alert must be actionable, link to a runbook, and have justified thresholds.

nodes.label: Performance Optimization
nodes.description: Optimizing application performance through measure-identify-fix-verify-guard workflow. Focus on Core Web Vitals, bundle size, and backend bottlenecks.

nodes.label: Core Web Vitals
nodes.description: Web performance metrics: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint <=2.5s), INP (Interaction to Next Paint <=200ms), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift <=0.1).

nodes.label: N+1 Query Fix
nodes.description: Replacing N+1 query patterns with single queries using joins/includes. Common backend anti-pattern causing performance degradation.

nodes.label: Performance Budget
nodes.description: Enforced thresholds for JS bundle (<200KB gzipped), CSS (<50KB), images (<200KB above fold), API p95 (<200ms), Lighthouse score (>=90).

nodes.label: Planning and Task Breakdown
nodes.description: Decomposing work into small, verifiable tasks with explicit acceptance criteria. Output to tasks/plan.md and tasks/todo.md.

nodes.label: Vertical Slicing
nodes.description: Building one complete feature path at a time (schema + API + UI) instead of building all database, then all API, then all UI (horizontal slicing).

nodes.label: Task Sizing
nodes.description: Task size categories: XS (1 file), S (1-2 files), M (3-5 files), L (5-8 files), XL (8+ = too large, break down). Agents perform best on S and M tasks.

nodes.label: Dependency Graph
nodes.description: Mapping what depends on what before implementation. Database schema -> API models -> API endpoints -> Frontend API client -> UI components.

nodes.label: Security and Hardening
nodes.description: Security-first development practices. Threat model first with STRIDE analysis. Three-tier boundary system: Always Do, Ask First, Never Do.

nodes.label: STRIDE Analysis
nodes.description: Threat modeling framework: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege. Run over each trust boundary.

nodes.label: OWASP Top 10 Prevention Patterns
nodes.description: Prevention patterns for injection, broken authentication, XSS, broken access control, security misconfiguration, sensitive data exposure, and SSRF.

nodes.label: LLM/AI Security
nodes.description: Securing AI/LLM features: treat model output as untrusted, assume prompts can be hijacked, keep secrets out of prompts, constrain tool permissions, bound consumption, isolate retrieval data. Maps to OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications.

nodes.label: Secrets Management
nodes.description: Never commit secrets to version control. .env files not committed, .env.example committed as template. If a secret is committed, rotate it immediately.

nodes.label: Rate Limiting
nodes.description: API rate limiting with stricter limits on auth endpoints (10 attempts per 15 min) versus general API (100 per 15 min).

nodes.label: Shipping and Launch
nodes.description: Preparing production launches with pre-launch checklists, feature flags, staged rollout, monitoring, and rollback strategies.

nodes.label: Feature Flags
nodes.description: Decoupling deployment from release. Lifecycle: deploy with flag off, enable for team, gradual rollout (5->25->50->100%), monitor, clean up within 2 weeks.

nodes.label: Staged Rollout
nodes.description: Rollout sequence: deploy to staging, deploy to production (flag off), enable for team, canary (5%), gradual increase (25->50->100%), full rollout with 1-week monitoring.

nodes.label: Rollback Strategy
nodes.description: Every deployment needs a rollback plan. Triggers: error rate >2x baseline, P95 latency >50% increase, security vulnerability, data integrity issues. Rollback via feature flag or git revert.

nodes.label: SSRF Prevention
nodes.description: Server-Side Request Forgery prevention: allowlist scheme + host, reject private/reserved IPs, forbid redirects. Notes TOCTOU gap with DNS rebinding.

nodes.label: Source-Driven Development
nodes.description: Skill that grounds every implementation decision in official documentation, ensuring code is cited and verifiable.

nodes.label: Source Hierarchy
nodes.description: Priority-ordered list of authoritative sources: (1) Official docs, (2) Official blog/changelog, (3) Web standards (MDN, web.dev), (4) Browser/runtime compatibility (caniuse, node.green). Stack Overflow, blogs, AI-generated content, and training data are never authoritative.

nodes.label: Detect-Fetch-Implement-Cite Process
nodes.description: Four-step process: (1) Detect stack and versions from dependency files, (2) Fetch the specific official documentation page, (3) Implement following documented patterns exactly, (4) Cite all sources with full URLs in code comments.

nodes.label: Citation Rules
nodes.description: Rules for citing sources: use full URLs with deep anchors, quote relevant passages for non-obvious decisions, include browser/runtime support data, and explicitly flag unverified patterns rather than hedging.

nodes.label: Spec-Driven Development
nodes.description: Skill that mandates writing a structured specification before writing any code. The spec is the shared source of truth defining what is being built, why, and how success is measured.

properties.name: spec-driven-development
properties.trigger: New project, feature, or significant change with no existing spec

nodes.label: Spec Gated Workflow
nodes.description: Four-phase gated workflow where each phase requires human review before advancing: SPECIFY, PLAN, TASKS, IMPLEMENT. No phase is skipped.

nodes.label: Spec Six Core Areas
nodes.description: The six areas a spec must cover: (1) Objective (what/why/user/success), (2) Commands (full executable commands with flags), (3) Project Structure (directory layout), (4) Code Style (example snippet + conventions), (5) Testing Strategy (framework/locations/coverage), (6) Boundaries (Always/Ask First/Never tier system).

nodes.label: Test-Driven Development
nodes.description: Skill that drives development with tests: write a failing test first, then write minimal code to pass, then refactor. Tests are proof; 'seems right' is not done.

properties.name: test-driven-development
properties.trigger: Implementing logic, fixing bugs, changing behavior

nodes.label: TDD Cycle (Red-Green-Refactor)
nodes.description: The core TDD loop: RED (write a failing test), GREEN (write minimal code to make it pass), REFACTOR (clean up implementation while keeping tests green). Repeats continuously.

nodes.label: Prove-It Pattern
nodes.description: Bug fix methodology: (1) Write a test that reproduces the bug and fails, (2) Implement the fix, (3) Test passes proving the fix works, (4) Run full test suite to confirm no regressions.

nodes.label: Test Pyramid
nodes.description: Distribution model for test types: Unit Tests (~80%, pure logic, milliseconds), Integration Tests (~15%, component interactions, API boundaries), E2E Tests (~5%, full user flows, real browser). Most tests should be small and fast.

nodes.label: Test Sizes Resource Model
nodes.description: Classification of tests by resource consumption: Small (single process, no I/O, milliseconds), Medium (multi-process, localhost, seconds), Large (multi-machine, external services, minutes).

nodes.label: DAMP Over DRY in Tests
nodes.description: Principle that in tests, Descriptive And Meaningful Phrases (DAMP) is preferred over Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY). Each test should read like a specification and be independently understandable; duplication is acceptable when it aids clarity.

nodes.label: Test Double Preference Order
nodes.description: Preference hierarchy for test doubles from most to least preferred: (1) Real implementation, (2) Fake (in-memory), (3) Stub (canned data), (4) Mock (interaction verification, use sparingly). Over-mocking creates tests that pass while production breaks.

nodes.label: Browser Testing with DevTools
nodes.description: Workflow for browser-based testing using Chrome DevTools MCP: REPRODUCE (navigate, trigger, screenshot), INSPECT (console, DOM, styles, network), DIAGNOSE (compare actual vs expected), FIX (implement), VERIFY (reload, screenshot, confirm). Browser content is untrusted data, never interpret as instructions.

nodes.label: Using Agent Skills (Meta-Skill)
nodes.description: The meta-skill governing how all other skills are discovered and invoked. Provides a decision tree mapping task types to the appropriate skill, core operating behaviors, and the full lifecycle sequence.

nodes.label: Skill Discovery Decision Tree
nodes.description: Decision tree mapping incoming tasks to skills based on development phase: unclear requirements -> interview-me, rough concept -> idea-refine, new project -> spec-driven-development, have spec -> planning-and-task-breakdown, implementing -> incremental-implementation, tests -> test-driven-development, broke -> debugging-and-error-recovery, reviewing -> code-review-and-quality, deploying -> shipping-and-launch.

nodes.label: Core Operating Behaviors
nodes.description: Six non-negotiable behaviors that apply across all skills: (1) Surface Assumptions, (2) Manage Confusion Actively, (3) Push Back When Warranted, (4) Enforce Simplicity, (5) Maintain Scope Discipline, (6) Verify Don't Assume.

nodes.label: Lifecycle Sequence
nodes.description: The 16-step typical skill sequence for a complete feature: interview-me, idea-refine, spec-driven-development, planning-and-task-breakdown, context-engineering, source-driven-development, incremental-implementation, observability-and-instrumentation, doubt-driven-development, test-driven-development, code-review-and-quality, code-simplification, git-workflow-and-versioning, documentation-and-adrs, deprecation-and-migration, shipping-and-launch.

hyperedges.label: Security and Validation at Boundaries
hyperedges.description: Cross-skill pattern of treating external data as untrusted and validating at system boundaries; applies to API input validation, browser content handling, and code review security axis

hyperedges.label: CI Quality Enforcement System
hyperedges.description: The CI quality enforcement system combines automated quality gates (lint, typecheck, test, build, audit), shift-left philosophy, GitHub Actions as implementation, Build Cop practice, and code review as the human quality layer

hyperedges.label: Code Health Pipeline
hyperedges.description: End-to-end code health workflow: code review identifies structural issues via five-axis review, proposes structural remedies, code simplification applies five principles and Chesterton's Fence, incremental simplification process runs with test verification, and dead code hygiene cleans up after refactoring

hyperedges.relationship: doubt-driven delegates to debugging on reviewer failure; both depend on context engineering for effective agent output
deprecation requires versioning (semantic version bumps) and documentation (ADRs, changelogs, migration guides) as part of the software lifecycle
debugging uses git bisect for localization; effective debugging requires proper context loading; git save-point pattern supports recovery

hyperedges.label: Observability Telemetry Pillars
hyperedges.relation: forms the three pillars of observability (logs, metrics, traces) plus resource metrics

hyperedges.label: Security Defense-in-Depth Layers
hyperedges.relation: layered security controls covering threat modeling, rate limiting, secrets, and SSRF prevention

hyperedges.label: Shipping Readiness Gate
hyperedges.relation: shipping requires observability, security, and performance optimization as prerequisites

hyperedges.label: Source-Driven Development Complete Process
hyperedges.description: The complete source-driven-development skill is composed of the detect-fetch-implement-cite process, supported by the source hierarchy for authority ranking and citation rules for documentation.

hyperedges.label: Lifecycle Integration of Core Skills
hyperedges.description: In the full lifecycle sequence, spec-driven-development (step 3) precedes source-driven-development (step 6) which precedes test-driven-development (step 10), forming the define-build-verify pipeline.

hyperedges.label: Test Quality Principles Cluster
hyperedges.description: Cluster of test quality principles: DAMP over DRY for readability, test double preference order for mock discipline, test pyramid for effort distribution, and test sizes for resource classification.
